era_context: The Roman Empire in the mid-3rd century crisis: the Severan dynasty has collapsed, and the throne is contested by multiple claimants. The empire faces simultaneous threats from Germanic tribes on the Rhine and Danube, Sasanid Persia in the east, and internal civil war. In China, the Han Dynasty has long fallen; the Three Kingdoms period (220–280 CE) is underway, with Cao Wei, Eastern Wu, and Shu Han competing for supremacy. In India, the Satavahana Empire is declining; regional powers are rising.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (archaeological evidence sparse for specific years)
Mesoamerica and South America (no written records for this period)

events.title: Maximinus Thrax proclaimed emperor; beginning of the Crisis of the Third Century
events.description: Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, a Thracian of humble origin and commander of the Legio III Cyrenaica, is proclaimed emperor by mutinous troops on the Rhine frontier after the assassination of Severus Alexander. He represents a break from the Severan dynasty and the beginning of fifty years of rapid imperial turnover and civil war. Maximinus is the first 'barracks emperor'—a soldier-emperor chosen by the legions rather than the Senate or imperial succession. His reign marks the start of the most destabilizing period in Roman imperial history.

sources.name: Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: The date and basic facts of Maximinus's accession are well-attested in multiple ancient sources, though written after the events. The year 235 CE is the conventional date accepted by modern scholarship.
cross_references: Leads to 238-001 (Year of the Six Emperors)

events.title: Maximinus Thrax launches campaigns against Germanic tribes on the Rhine and Danube
events.description: Maximinus, seeking to consolidate his power and address the chronic threat of Germanic incursions, launches major military campaigns against the Alamanni on the Rhine and the Goths on the Danube. These campaigns are initially successful and boost his popularity with the legions, but they are costly and require heavy taxation of the provinces. The military pressure from Germanic tribes is relentless and will define the remainder of the 3rd century.
key_figures: Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus (emperor and military commander)

sources.name: Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: The campaigns are attested in ancient sources, but precise dating within Maximinus's reign (235–238) is uncertain. The year 235 is assigned by convention as the beginning of his military activity.
cross_references: Part of broader Germanic pressure on Rome (ongoing through 3rd century)

events.title: Cao Pi of Cao Wei dies; Cao Rui becomes ruler of the Wei state
events.description: Cao Pi, the first emperor of the Cao Wei state (one of the Three Kingdoms), dies after a reign marked by consolidation of northern Chinese territory and military campaigns against rival states. He is succeeded by his son Cao Rui, who continues Wei's dominance in the north. This succession is significant for the stability it brings to the Wei state during a period of fragmentation. Cao Rui will rule until 239 CE and is remembered as an effective administrator.
key_figures: Cao Pi (died 226 CE, but succession effects continue)
Cao Rui (new ruler of Cao Wei)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms) by Chen Shou
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Cao Rui's accession is well-documented in Chinese historical records. However, Cao Pi died in 226 CE, not 235 CE. This entry may be misplaced; Cao Rui was already ruling in 235. Correction: Cao Rui was ruling from 226–239 CE, so 235 falls within his reign, not a succession year.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220–280 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The Historia Augusta, a primary source for Maximinus Thrax, is now recognized by modern scholarship as containing significant legendary and fabricated material, particularly regarding Maximinus's origins and early life. Some scholars question whether Maximinus was as militarily successful as portrayed. Additionally, the exact date of Maximinus's accession (235 CE) is based on later sources; contemporary documentation is minimal. The entry regarding Cao Pi/Cao Rui contains a chronological error: Cao Pi died in 226 CE, not 235 CE, so this is not an event of 235 itself.
historiographic_note: The year 235 CE marks a major historiographic turning point in Roman history, but the sources are problematic. The Historia Augusta is the primary narrative source and is demonstrably unreliable. Herodian provides a more contemporary account but wrote decades after the events. For China, the Sanguozhi is a near-contemporary chronicle compiled within a century of the Three Kingdoms period, making it more reliable than Roman sources. The Roman record is heavily biased toward court narratives and military perspectives; the experience of provincial populations is largely absent. Chinese sources similarly reflect the perspective of the ruling courts rather than common people.

graph_edges.note: Maximinus's reign and unpopularity led directly to the Year of the Six Emperors in 238 CE
Assassination of Severus Alexander triggered Maximinus's accession
Germanic pressure on Roman frontiers continued throughout the 3rd century crisis
